page: Vultr vx1-m-192c-1536gb is a memory-optimized virtual server belonging to the VX1 Cloud Compute family. It features an x86_64 architecture powered by an AMD processor with 192 vCPUs and 192 physical cores. The system configuration allocates 1.0 thread per core, resulting in no hyperthreading. It is equipped with 1435.0 GB of system memory, providing approximately 7.47 GB of RAM per core.

This server utilizes a shared CPU allocation model, which is a notable architectural tradeoff for an instance of this scale. Local storage is limited to a 1 GB network-attached volume, meaning primary data storage must be handled via external network storage systems. The instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address and does not feature any GPU acceleration.

Given the shared CPU allocation and the massive 1435.0 GB memory capacity, this instance offers a qualitative cost-efficiency advantage for memory-intensive workloads that do not require sustained, dedicated single-core CPU performance. It is designed for large-scale memory-resident databases, caching layers, in-memory analytics, and batch processing tasks where high RAM density is prioritized over dedicated CPU execution.
description: Vultr vx1-m-192c-1536gb is an x86_64 virtual server within the VX1 Cloud Compute family, configured with 192 AMD vCPUs and 1435.0 GB of RAM. Operating with a shared CPU allocation and a single thread per physical core, this instance offers high memory density at approximately 7.47 GB per core. It lacks GPU hardware and provides a minimal 1 GB network-based local storage volume, requiring external storage configurations for larger datasets. This resource profile is structured for memory-intensive applications, such as large-scale caching, in-memory databases, and data processing tasks that benefit from massive RAM capacity without requiring dedicated, non-shared CPU execution.
og_description: Vultr vx1-m-192c-1536gb is a shared-CPU virtual server featuring 192 AMD vCPUs, 1435.0 GB of RAM, and 1 GB of network storage, designed specifically for memory-intensive workloads and large-scale caching applications.
